era_context: The Crisis of the Third Century is at its height. The Roman Empire is fragmented into three competing power centers: the central state under Gallienus, the Gallic Empire in the west, and the Palmyrene Empire in the east. Gallienus is struggling to maintain control. Odaenathus is at the height of his power as a Palmyrene king and Roman ally. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is in its final phase. The Sassanid Persian Empire is stable and powerful.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier accounts

events.title: Odaenathus at the height of his power as Palmyrene king
events.description: In 266 CE, Odaenathus was at the height of his power as king of Palmyra. He had risen to prominence as a Roman ally against the Sassanid Persians and had expanded Palmyrene control over Egypt and much of Anatolia. Odaenathus maintained the nominal fiction of loyalty to Rome while exercising autonomous rule. He was a skilled military commander and administrator, and his court was a center of Hellenistic culture. However, his power was based on personal military success and political acumen, and it would not survive his death.
events.certainty_note: Odaenathus' power and prominence in 266 CE are well-attested by numismatic and epigraphic evidence. The extent of his territorial control and his relationship with Rome are debated.

events.title: Gallienus continues to face military and political challenges
events.description: In 266 CE, Gallienus (r. 253–268 CE) continued to face multiple military and political challenges. The Goths and other Germanic tribes threatened the Danube frontier. The Gallic Empire controlled the western provinces. The Palmyrene Empire was expanding in the east. Gallienus' reign is traditionally viewed as a period of decline, though modern scholars debate the extent of his actual incompetence. His military reforms, including the creation of a mobile cavalry force, were significant innovations.
events.certainty_note: Gallienus' difficulties in 266 CE are attested by multiple sources, but the specific events and their significance are debated.

events.title: Gallic Empire maintains control of western provinces
events.description: The Gallic Empire, established in 260 CE, continued to function as an independent state in 266 CE. The exact ruler is debated (possibly Postumus or his successor), but the Gallic Empire maintained control over Gaul, Britannia, and Hispania. The Gallic Empire minted coins, maintained an army, and administered the western provinces as a separate political entity.
key_figures: Postumus or successor (emperor of Gallic Empire)
events.certainty_note: The existence of the Gallic Empire in 266 CE is secure, but the exact ruler and specific events are uncertain.

events.title: Western Jin Dynasty consolidates power in China
events.description: The Western Jin Dynasty was consolidating power in 266 CE. Emperor Wu (Sima Yan) was establishing administrative structures and preparing for the final conquest of the southern kingdoms. The period was marked by relative stability and military preparation.
events.certainty_note: The Western Jin Dynasty's existence and general chronology are well-established in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Shapur I or successor
events.description: The Sassanid Persian Empire remained stable and powerful in 266 CE. The exact ruler is debated (possibly Shapur I, Hormizd I, or Bahram I, depending on chronological system), but the empire maintained control over its territories and benefited from Rome's internal crisis. The Sassanid state was one of the few stable great powers during this period.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The Sassanid Empire's stability is clear, but the exact ruler and specific events of 266 CE are uncertain due to disputed chronologies.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Odaenathus as a loyal Roman ally is complicated by evidence suggesting he may have pursued independent ambitions. The extent of his territorial control and his relationship with Rome are debated by modern scholars. The characterization of Gallienus as an incompetent ruler is challenged by modern scholars who emphasize his military innovations. The Gallic Empire's exact chronology and succession is disputed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 266 CE is heavily dependent on later compilations, particularly Historia Augusta, which is known to be unreliable. Contemporary sources are sparse: numismatic evidence (coins) and epigraphic evidence (inscriptions) provide the most reliable data. The narrative is heavily weighted toward the Roman perspective. Chinese sources for this period are more reliable and detailed. The Sassanid record is fragmentary. The overall picture is one of imperial fragmentation and military crisis, but specific events and their dating are often uncertain.

graph_edges.note: Odaenathus' power in 266 CE was followed by his death in 267 CE
Gallienus' challenges in 266 CE continued into 267 CE
